speaker: interviewer
text: --but if we're around two hours. So-- OK. So you think 12:00? Yeah, I think that would be better. 12:00 If it's later than that, then I'll-- I'll give you a call or you can just sit here. OK. OK, so what-- why don't you call him and just tell him to be in at 12:00? OK. They will-- And just rolling a little bit of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Francene Rotenberg. Today's date is December 21, 1995. I am interviewing Geinia Nornberg Weinstein in the city of New Haven, Connecticut, in the United States of America. And the interview is conducted in English. OK. Can you tell us your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Geinia Weinstein-- G-E-I-N-E-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably should have said on-- they thought that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I know. And your name at birth? What did they call you and your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Geineia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nornberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you spell Nornberg for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-O-R-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hebrew names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a Yiddish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's a Jewish name. And when was your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you now? How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now 81-- my, God!

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Brzesko Nove. You want that I spell?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-Z-E-S-K-O, N-O-V-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your town that you lived in before the war with your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, before the war, a very small town. And over there, I went in the school not too long. Then we moved to Przoszece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R-Z-O-S-Z-E-C-E, Przoszece.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Over there I went into school. Once a week I worked in store. You know, because it wasn't so good. I have to make a living. I go in the school and once a week in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your dad do? What-- what-- what was his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I sell it-- sales.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a special from the men-- the shoes-- not the boots. Yeah. He was a special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you an orthodox family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not orthodox, but we keep kosher, strictly kosher. My mother was very kosher. And everyone go in the shul and davened every day. Oh, yeah. It's not strictly like now the Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Shabbes is Shabbes. You make Shabbes and light the candles, you know, and everything. And they go in the shul, the men. It was like this in our small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what Shabbes was like. Did your mother bake?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's like this. We work. I worked in a house then in-- in-- then in Krakow, not in-- and it was before Shabbes, everything is cleaned; make challahs, wine, fish has to be every week-- fish-- no, and chicken soup, and the like, you know. Everything is like this in our small town. And when they came from the shul, then we eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my brother. And I wasn't married this time. I was young. And this was in next day Shabbes, they went to the shul. We didn't work. No. And we didn't cook in Shabbes-- no. Oh, it's like this. Then after the meal, we go to this certain town. And that's the whole day it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home I speak Yiddish and Polish-- Yiddish mostly, and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were little, they all spoke Yiddish at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Polish, too. You know, a lot of people, they couldn't talk really Pole. They just-- it's very difficult. Yeah. Polish language is very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends who weren't Jewish or-- when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then in the school I have friends, you know-- Jewish and Polish. I was-- I was very close to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience any anti-Semitism when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. When I was young, I don't remember this. And then when the war came, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen? When did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- when--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what were the first signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came-- not from-- from-- Przoszece. We moved to Krakow. In Krakow--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, I used to work by myself. And I got one girl, one machine, there was two machines. We work very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not married.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived with-- who did you live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the girl was-- lived by us, too, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a relative or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know her from the small town. I bring her to Krakow, and she works with me the whole time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year it was? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, about two, three years before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 1936, '35-- something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- when the war was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war came--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it come? How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hear. Oh, yeah. When they was in Poland, we know, because we know everything. The-- the-- the bombs-- everything. And they came, right away. You couldn't find anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have warning before the bombs fell?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just one day--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- like this, just like this. And we-- no bread-- nothing. There was a big, big line. The Poland stays, too. They take you from the line and throw you out. And you have to go again and try, you know. It was very, very hard to find some things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I got a sister in-- in small town, Brzeko. Nowe. She lives over there. She got a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tobel Raizel. But I don't remember the name for her-- from her husband. I don't. And we went over there to her. When we went-- yeah-- when the war-- then was in 1939. And I says to my first husband, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Gams.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says to him, you know what? I can't go. I'm not gonna leave my parents. I staying with my parents and you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where he want. Everybody left on Lvov. Lvov. This was in the Russia-- us belong with-- for-- to Poland-- you know, Lvov. But they went over there. He was over there. And then my brother, my youngest brother, was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe. So he was over there, and they saw each other. Then they lived together by Ukraine. And she-- they used to work. This was a long, long time when they wanna come back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, for what? They wanna send him to Russia to bury and to this. He says, no. I want to go. He want to go home. He left me and my parent.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one day. The Ukrainian got one day they can do with the Jewish people what they want. She bringed the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- the [NON-ENGLISH] to the room. She said she has Jewish people. And then the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a superintendent. This is like she take care of the house, you know, everything. So she bringed everybody over there, the Ukrainian. They got the one day they can do with the Jewish people. What they did-- my oldest brother was over there, too, but not in the same place. Then he talks from them to-- he take his head on the wall. He couldn't hear. What they did, they cut pieces from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was in Israel, but he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and he saw this and told you? Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's how I know. And they got killed by them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were living though in Krakow with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah but then we left to the small town to my sister over there. We-- we was thinking maybe they not coming over there. But they was over there-- all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then when you were living? How did you live with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard-- not so easy. I don't know how many-- two months. And what I got everything-- and my brother, I give everything to one guy. He was the chief from the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he tell us, this night, you have to go from the town. Because they are going here. And we left, and the small kids, my sister's three small kids, my mother, my father, and I. We didn't-- where are you gonna go? No place. Who will let you in? Nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one guy let us in where they caught the-- the horses, the cow-- one night. And then in the night we hear what was going on-- the small town. They was killed. This family was killed-- everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the policeman who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the person who took you in that night, that one night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was in our village-- in our village. Next day, he says, you have to go. Where we go? No place to go. We go again to one. I told you, my father got the money. He borrowed then the money. And he let him in-- but not for long-- just the same place where the cow-- the cow, and the-- and the-- and the horses, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my mother says to me, you know what? Don't stay with us. You go. You go. I say, where I go? I don't know where to go. It was one guy over there, Polish guy. He says, I take her to Krakow in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got my brother-- the oldest brother was in ghetto. This time, the ghetto was closed. You can't go-- not go-- not in, not out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like? What did it look like, the ghetto? Did it have a wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All the Jewish people in one place-- it was a big place, you know, the-- not so big, but all the Jewish people was in the one place. And I says-- and the guy says, yeah. If you give me 3,000 zloty-- not dollars-- 3,000 zloty, I take her to Krakow. No. When you got to Krakow with me, I don't know you-- nothing. You-- you go first, or I go first, and you go behind. It was 40 kilometers to Krakow from this village. So my father says, I have $20 I can give you-- no more. I don't have any more. So he says, no. I wanted 3,000 zlotys. The $20 he doesn't want.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father gives him the 3,000 zloty, and he went with me. But I was afraid, you know, that the German they go in and back-- they recognize me or not. So I take something, a piece of bread, another [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how-- on the back-- and-- and milk. And I put a-- a scarf like this. Nobody recognized me. But I couldn't walk so many kilometers. You know, I says, I got like this. I says, you know what? Maybe we take the-- it was a tram by in Poland. He said-- I says, I can't walk anymore. He says, what? You crazy? If somebody recognize you-- and this was true. And I said, you know what? [NON-ENGLISH] was at the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was like here, sometimes here. Sometime you need a [NON-ENGLISH] another wagon. A [NON-ENGLISH] is a horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, a horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a carriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a-- yeah. But-- but this was covered. And he did. So where can I go? You can't go in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I says, you know what? In this same strip, where the ghetto was, my brother used to work-- live on this street. I says, take me over there to the superintendent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me. See, he got two kids. She's saw me. Oh! You know how the Pollacks-- I'm afraid, you know. But the kids are not here. I take you and put you in the closet. And-- but if the kids enter here, they cannot recognize you. They can go and tell them. I was two days in-- in the closet. And then I says, go and tell my brother that I am here. He was sure nobody's alive, because he-- he knows everybody. And they hear the-- the bum! Bombs! And here. I am here. He says, listen. Keep her the night, but I gonna try and tell one from the other man. This was the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this again? Was it Simcha?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest, Sigmund.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sigmund. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the oldest. So he says, I take her. I can't take her now. It has to be legal, you now? And before, when the ghetto is in the door, was a other German. He was so good. He was killed in-- before the war-- about two days before. Yeah. But he got a-- a Jewish woman, and he keep her in someplace with the-- with her kids-- a 3-- 3-years-old boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the German's name-- the Guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. He was-- he didn't see nothing. When you go to work outside, they-- the people, they bring bread, you know. But he didn't see. He covered the Jewish woman. And her husband was with me together in Brunnlitz-- the woman's husband. Before the war, somebody saw him, tell the German here and here is a Jewish woman. He put her here. They killed-- killed him. They killed her, and the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were locked in the closet for two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two days. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- you-- were you able to eat or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: She given me, yeah. Because-- and then the kids, they went to the school. They were not at home. So she give me, yeah-- about two nights. I was so quiet. I couldn't say nothing, you know. And she was happy when the kids, they go out. When I came to around other man. This is the Jewish police, you know. He take me in-- and let me in-- the German. He let me in-- not even to my brother. My sister-in-law, what I got, I says, better than when I was in my town. And I was killed. Must help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't see me. She doesn't want to see me here. Then you can go like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to work. You have to work. If I have to go to work, I have to have a machine to-- a sewing machine. And I didn't got-- on where? Tell me, your sister-in-law said you had to go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't-- no. You-- when you was in ghetto, you have to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you you had to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody has to work! If not, they killed you! So my brother got somebody in Madritsch where I-- they take me over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there it was the Jewish people are sewing. This was the uniform what I told--

speaker: interviewer
text: But who took you to Madritsch? Were they Germans, or they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah! You know the-- the-- the people that he knows.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he takes me over there, and I was over there. They didn't know I can do some things or not. So they try.

speaker: interviewee
text: They give me first, I remember like now, the collars for men's shirts. The collars was for Madritsch-- on-- the chef from the factory. And I make the collars. One looks at the one that says, oh, beautiful, so nice. They couldn't believe that they-- I sit in the first [NON-ENGLISH]. And I got a machine. I got everything. And the supervisors-- this was Jewish people. And then they give me, you know, pyjamas to do for Madritsch. I did. I was the first over there-- the first. They need something, they came to me, you know. And for a while, we was-- we lived in-- in ghetto, and we go to outside. This was not too far from ghetto. From ghetto to Madritsch was in Krakow. And we went over there. But now a few months--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this? A year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the year-- 1939 or '40-- '40. And we went over there. And then from Madritsch, then he got six shirts to make for [NON-ENGLISH]. If these-- if some stitch is not right, he can kill you. He looks like this on-- you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was this [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was the ship-- the chef from the whole concentration camp Krakow-Plaszow. And we went-- before we went to work in Krakow-Plaszow. We didn't go when they liquidated this ghetto, you know. So we went to Krakow-Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they liquidate the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- 1940-- '41? Exactly, I don't remember the date. When we came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you? How did you know they were liquidating? Did someone--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have a radio or--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no! They came. And then everybody has to go in concentration camp-- no more ghetto. In ghetto when we was-- when it was-- there's a liquidate the ghetto, got to over there. It was unbelievable what they doing with the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about what you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: They kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I saw-- I was scared to look. I saw this. They take a baby, a small baby from third floor. This is not the third floor of like here. This is the second, third. This is like here the fourth floor. They ripped in the hand and they throw down. This I saw in ghetto when they liquidated. Yeah. They killed them. They killed them. They go house by house. They look. Or somebody is not inside, who was inside, they kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Germans or Polish?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: German, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.
